clean_story:
Vibrations gone wild. Structures self-destructing.

The term resonance disaster describes when rhythmic forces—like wind, footsteps, or engine pulses—match an object’s natural vibration frequency, amplifying energy until it tears itself apart. It’s like pushing a swing at the exact right moment, again and again, until the chains snap.

Every material hums with a hidden frequency: bridges, buildings, even wine glasses. When an outside rhythm syncs with this hum, the object absorbs more energy than it can handle. The Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapsed in 1940 after 40-mile-per-hour winds matched its wobble, twisting it like a ribbon until steel cables failed. Engineers later calculated its natural frequency: 0.2 Hertz—one full sway every five seconds. Soldiers marching in unison once destroyed a French bridge in 1850; their boots hit the wood’s resonance, splintering planks mid-stride.

Prevention relies on dampers (shock absorbers for buildings) or tuning structures to avoid deadly harmonics. Skyscrapers use pendulums weighing 300 to 800 tons to counteract sway. Yet risks linger: in 2000, London’s Millennium Bridge wobbled violently when crowds walked in sync, forcing a $9 million fix.

Three related subjects are bridge collapse physics, musical glass shattering, and earthquake-proof design.
